chunk_summary: Responsive feeding emphasizes recognizing hunger and satiety cues, avoiding coercion, and fostering healthy mealtime behaviors in infants and toddlers under 2 years to support growth and appetite regulation.
learning_objectives: Identify hunger and satiety cues in infants and toddlers during feeding.
Understand the importance of non-coercive feeding practices for healthy appetite regulation.
Implement structured mealtime routines to promote responsive feeding.
Recognize the role of responsive feeding in addressing stunting, anemia, and overweight in early childhood.
key_facts: Hunger cues include rooting, hand-to-mouth movements, leaning forward, and excitement at mealtime.
Satiety cues include slowing pace, turning away, spitting out food, or pushing the spoon away.
Meals should ideally last 20–30 minutes; prolonged conflict should be avoided.
Positioning the child upright and maintaining eye contact during feeding supports safe swallowing.
Responsive feeding is critical in India, where stunting (low height-for-age) and anemia coexist with rising overweight rates.
reference_quote: Responsive feeding supports healthy appetite regulation while permitting therapeutic 'energy boosting' when growth falters. (WHO Guiding Principles for Complementary Feeding of the Breastfed Child)
document_key_topics: Hunger and satiety cues in infants and toddlers
Impact of feeding practices on growth and nutrition in early childhood
